body:
IDPicker has five distinct user roles, each with a specific set of capabilities. Understanding what each role can and cannot do helps you set up accounts correctly and avoid confusion about access rights.
1. The Five Roles at a Glance Role Who holds it Primary purpose Super Admin Platform owner / IT operations staff Full control of technical settings, all organisations, global content, and billing Advisor Head counselor or primary school contact Manages their student roster, runs forms, distributes resources, chats with families Sub-Advisor Assistant counselor or specialist staff Operates within a restricted subset of the Advisor’s permissions and student scope Student The young person using the platform Completes assessments, explores recommendations, chats with their advisor and AI mentor Parent / Guardian Parent or legal guardian of a student Monitors a linked child’s progress and communicates with the advisor 2. Permission Comparison Table Students and Core Assessment Action Super Admin Advisor Sub-Advisor Student Parent Take self-assessments — — — ✅ — View own assessment results — — — ✅ Read-only view View any student’s results ✅ ✅ Scoped only — Own child only Edit another user’s answers ✅ — — — — Forms and Documents Action Super Admin Advisor Sub-Advisor Student Parent Create global forms ✅ — — — — Create organisation forms — ✅ If permitted — — Submit a form response — — — ✅ ✅ View form responses ✅ ✅ If permitted Own only — Upload application documents — — — ✅ — Review / verify documents ✅ ✅ If permitted — — Communication Action Super Admin Advisor Sub-Advisor Student Parent Send notifications to all students ✅ — — — — Send notifications to own cohort — ✅ If permitted — — Chat with their advisor — — — ✅ ✅ Chat with a student’s parent — ✅ ✅ — — Join a family group chat — ✅ ✅ ✅ ✅ User and Organisation Management Action Super Admin Advisor Sub-Advisor Student Parent Create organisations ✅ — — — — Invite advisors ✅ — — — — Invite sub-advisors — ✅ — — — Import students in bulk — ✅ If permitted — — Link a parent to a student ✅ — — — Request only Delete any account ✅ — — — — AI and Content Action Super Admin Advisor Sub-Advisor Student Parent Edit global AI system prompts ✅ — — — — Edit organisation-level prompts — ✅ — — — Use AI mentor chat — — — ✅ — Issue certificates — ✅ If permitted — — Generate a Journey Map PDF ✅ — — ✅ ✅ 3. How Sub-Advisor Permissions Work Sub-Advisors do not have a fixed permission set. Their access is configured entirely by their primary Advisor using the Permission Matrix.
An Advisor can grant a Sub-Advisor access to any combination of modules they themselves hold. An Advisor cannot grant a Sub-Advisor more rights than the Advisor has. Sub-Advisors can also be scoped to a specific student group — for example, only the students in “Class of 2026” — so they cannot see any other students even within the same organisation. Example: An Advisor might create a Sub-Advisor account for an office assistant, giving them access only to Forms and Notifications but no access to Students, Groups, or Support Tickets.
4. How Parent Access Works Parents have a deliberately limited view to protect student privacy:
A parent can only see data for students they are explicitly linked to. The student controls the link — they must approve a parent’s request from their own dashboard. Parents can view progress, recommendations, and Journey Maps, but they cannot edit anything. In schools where Parent Chat is enabled, parents can message the advisor through a secure thread that is separate from the student-advisor channel. Image placeholder: Add a diagram showing the five roles and the directional relationships between them \u2014 e.g., Admin oversees all, Advisor manages Sub-Advisor and Students, Parent links to Student.
Common Questions Situation What to do A sub-advisor says they cannot see a specific student Check their Assigned Scope in Sub-Advisor Management — they may only be scoped to certain groups, and the student is in a different group A parent can see their child but cannot chat with the advisor Family Chat must be enabled by the Advisor. The advisor can open the Chat module and confirm the parent thread is active I need to give an advisor admin-level access to one feature only Use the Sub-Admin (Admin-level) permission matrix rather than making them a full Super Admin

body:
Everything students need to create an account, sign in, and recover access starts here. Follow each section from top to bottom the first time through; returning users can jump directly to the relevant flow.
1. Before You Begin Make sure you have:
The student login URL for your environment — see Access URLs A valid email address you can check immediately A stable internet connection and an up-to-date browser 2. Sign In with Email and Password This is the fastest path for returning students.
Open the student login URL. Click the Email field and type your registered email. Click the Password field and type your password. (Optional) Click the eye icon next to the password field to reveal what you typed. Click Sign In and wait for the redirect. What happens next:
You land on your Dashboard. If you were navigating to a specific page before login, you are sent directly there instead. If sign-in fails:
Double-check for typos — passwords are case-sensitive. If you see a message about email verification, follow the Verify Your Email section below. If you have forgotten your password, use Forgot Password. 3. Sign In with Google Faster sign-in without a separate password.
Open the student login page. Click Continue with Google. A Google account picker opens — select the account you want to use. Approve the consent screen if prompted. You are redirected back to IDPicker and land on the dashboard. If the login window stays on the same page:
Your browser may be blocking popups. Allow popups for this site and try again. Make sure you are not using a private/incognito window with strict tracking prevention. 4. Register a New Account New students follow a multi-step wizard. The exact steps depend on whether you received an invitation link.
Click Sign Up on the login page. Fill in your name, email, and a strong password. Password must contain at least one uppercase letter, one number, and one special character. Confirm the password and check the consent checkbox. Complete the interests and goals step. Fill out your profile (education level, gender, social links, occupation). Select an organization if prompted, or continue with invitation-prefilled values. Complete the human verification step. Click the final Register button. Tip: If you were invited by an advisor, your organization field may already be filled in. Do not change it unless instructed.
Common registration blockers:
Duplicate email → use a different address or try Forgot Password if you already registered. Consent checkbox not checked → scroll down and tick the box. Weak password → make sure it meets all strength requirements shown on screen. 5. Verify Your Email After registration, IDPicker sends a verification email.
Open your email inbox (check your spam/junk folder too). Find the email from IDPicker with subject Verify your email. Click the verification link inside. Wait for the verification confirmation page. Once verified, you are directed to your dashboard or profile completion. If the link says it has expired:
Return to the login page. Enter your email and click Resend Verification Email. Open the new email and click the fresh link. 6. Forgot Password Use this if you cannot remember your current password.
Open the login page and click Forgot Password. Enter your registered email address. Click Send Reset Link. Check your inbox and spam folder for the reset email. 7. Reset Password From the Email Link After requesting a reset, you will receive a link by email.
Click the link from the IDPicker reset email. Wait a moment for the token to be validated. Enter your new password. Enter the same password again to confirm. Watch the strength indicator — make sure it reaches acceptable strength. Click Reset Password. Return to the login page and sign in with your new password. If the link says the token is invalid or expired:
Request a new reset link from the Forgot Password step above. Common Questions Situation What to do Correct password rejected Likely unverified email — resend verification from the login page Google login popup closed immediately Enable popups for the IDPicker domain in your browser settings Reset link opens but password field is grayed out Token expired — request a fresh reset link Registration stuck on verification Check spam folder; if email is over 30 minutes old, resend

body:
IDPicker is a career and university guidance platform that helps students discover their strengths, explore academic pathways, and receive AI-powered recommendations — all within a supervised ecosystem connecting students, advisors, and parents.
1. What IDPicker Does IDPicker guides students through a structured self-discovery and decision-making journey using three core engines:
Engine What it does Psychometric Assessments Measures personality traits, aptitudes, and interests using validated questionnaires AI Recommendations Matches universities and career programmes to each student’s unique profile AI Mentorship Provides on-demand guidance conversations powered by the student’s own assessment data The platform is used by individual students, school-based advisors managing hundreds of students, parents tracking their child’s progress, and organizational super-admins governing the entire deployment.
Image placeholder: Add a simple workflow diagram showing the student journey: Register → Assessment → Personality → Recommendations → AI Mentor.
2. The Five User Roles Every account on IDPicker belongs to exactly one role. Each role has its own login panel and access scope.
Role Who they are What they can do Student The primary end-user Take assessments, view personality profile, get recommendations, use AI mentor, upload documents Advisor Teacher, counselor, or guidance staff Manage student rosters, assign forms, issue certificates, run family chats, configure sub-advisors Sub-Advisor Assistant to a primary advisor A scoped-down advisor with access only to the students and modules the primary advisor grants Parent / Guardian A student’s family member View linked child’s progress, chat with advisor, download Journey Map Admin Platform operator or school IT lead Configure organizations, manage global assessments and AI prompts, monitor usage and system health Note: A person can only hold one role per account. If a teacher is also a parent, they need two separate accounts registered with different email addresses.
3. The Student Journey The typical student experience follows this sequence:
Register — Create an account or accept an advisor’s invitation link. Complete Assessment — Answer the psychometric questionnaire (usually 15–30 minutes). Review Personality — Explore the radar chart and trait cards generated from the assessment. Get Recommendations — Refine the AI shortlist using exam scores or personality mode. Chat with AI Mentor — Ask the persona-based mentor questions about career paths and university choices. Search Universities — Browse and filter the university database directly. Upload Documents — Submit required application documents through the secure document tracker. Download Report — Export a full recommendations or personality PDF for external use. 4. How Advisors, Students, and Parents Connect IDPicker is designed as a supervised platform. Relationships between roles are explicit and governed:
Advisor → Student: An advisor invites students by email, or students register under the advisor’s organization link. The advisor can see all assigned students’ progress. Student → Parent: The student approves or denies a parent link request. No parent can access a student’s data without the student’s consent. Advisor → Sub-Advisor: The primary advisor creates sub-advisor accounts, assigns specific student groups to them, and controls which modules they can access. Admin → All: Platform admins have global visibility across all organizations for support and governance. Image placeholder: Add a simple diagram showing how advisor, student, and parent accounts link together with arrows indicating who initiates each connection.
5. Two Types of Student Accounts Type How it works Organization Student Registered under a school or agency. The advisor manages their account and progress is visible to the organization. Independent Student Registered directly (not through an organization). No advisor oversight. Subscription-based features apply directly. Common Questions Situation What to do I am not sure which panel to use Your invitation email or onboarding link will take you directly to the correct panel for your role I forgot my login URL See the Access URLs page for all environment-specific login addresses I need to act as both advisor and parent Register two separate accounts with different email addresses — one for each role

body:
The Dashboard is the first thing you see after logging in. It gives you a bird’s-eye view of everything that needs your attention — assessments, notifications, forms, invitations, and program recommendations.
1. What You See on the Dashboard When you land on the dashboard, you will see several content blocks:
Block What It Shows What to Do Assessment Reports Your latest completed assessment output Download or view your report Assigned Forms Forms sent to you by your advisor or organization Open and respond Organization Invitations Pending invitations to join an organization Accept or Decline Notifications System and advisor announcements Read and act on relevant items Promoted Programs Highlighted university/career programs Browse and explore Random Programs Exploratory program discovery Click into any that look interesting 2. Your First 5 Actions After Logging In Follow this sequence whenever you start a new session for maximum efficiency:
Check Notifications — Look for any new announcements from your advisor or platform. Open Assigned Forms — If a form is pending, complete it now before it slips away. Resume an Assessment — If you left an assessment in progress, the dashboard shows a resume prompt. Browse Recommendations — Click Recommendations in the sidebar to check your latest career or university matches. Explore Program Cards — Browse promoted and random programs to discover new options. 3. Using the Assessment Reports Block Locate the Assessment Reports block on your dashboard. If a report is available, click the View or Download button. If the button appears locked or restricted: Your assessment may not be complete yet → go to Self Assessment in the sidebar. Your account plan may not include report downloads → check your subscription in Settings. 4. Using the Assigned Forms Block Forms are created by your advisor and require your response.
Locate Assigned Forms on the dashboard. Click Open on any pending form. Complete all fields on the form page. Click Submit. You are returned to the dashboard and the form disappears from the pending list. Note: Submitted forms are removed automatically from the pending list. If a form disappears, it was already submitted or removed by the advisor.
5. Handling Organization Invitations Find the Invitations block on the dashboard. Click an invitation card to review the organization name and context. Click Accept to join or Decline to refuse. Your decision is saved immediately. 6. Reading Notifications Locate the Notifications block. Click on any notification that looks relevant. It may take you directly to the related module (form, assessment, recommendation, etc.). After reviewing all, return to the main dashboard. 7. Resuming an In-Progress Assessment If you started an assessment and did not finish it:
The dashboard shows a Resume prompt card automatically. Click Resume Assessment. You are taken back to exactly where you left off. Complete all remaining questions and click Submit. 8. Exploring Program Cards Promoted programs are specifically featured by the platform or your organization. Random programs are surfaced for open exploration. Click any program card to view its details. To search and filter more precisely, open University Search from the sidebar.
Common Questions Situation What to do Dashboard appears empty on first login Normal — data populates as you complete assessments, receive forms, and get invitations A form I need is missing Check with your advisor — they may not have assigned it yet, or you may have already submitted it Report download button is greyed out Complete your assessment first, or check your subscription plan Recommendations block not visible It may only appear after your first assessment is complete

body:
This guide is for brand-new users — students, advisors, and parents — who want to get from zero to their first meaningful action in under 15 minutes. Follow the section that matches your role.
1. Opening the Right Login Page IDPicker has separate login portals for each role. Before anything else, make sure you are on the correct URL.
Open your browser and navigate to the login address provided by your school, organization, or invitation email. Confirm the page title matches your role (e.g., Student Login, Advisor Login). If you are unsure of the correct URL, see the Access URLs page. Tip: Bookmark your login page the first time you use it. There are multiple portal addresses and going to the wrong one will prevent sign-in.
Image placeholder: Add screenshot of the login page with the browser address bar visible showing the correct URL.
2. Signing In or Creating Your Account If you already have an account Enter your registered Email and Password. Click Sign In. If your organization supports it, click Continue with Google instead for one-click access. If you are a new student registering yourself Click Sign Up on the login page. Complete the multi-step wizard: name, email, password, interests, and profile. Check your inbox for the verification email and click the link inside. Return to the login page and sign in. If you received an invitation link Open the invitation email from IDPicker. Click the Setup Password button or link. Set a strong password and confirm it. You are automatically signed in and placed in the correct panel. Image placeholder: Add screenshot of the completed sign-in form with both email login and Google login options visible.
3. Student First Run: Assessment → Personality → Recommendations Step 1 — Complete your assessment After signing in, you land on the Student Dashboard. Find the Self Assessment card or click Self Assessment in the sidebar. Click Start on the available assessment. Answer every question honestly — there are no wrong answers. Click Submit when you finish. Tip: The assessment takes 15–30 minutes. Find a quiet moment to complete it — your first instinct answers are always the most accurate.
Image placeholder: Add screenshot of the student dashboard with the assessment card highlighted and Start button visible.
Step 2 — Explore your Personality profile After submission, click Personality in the sidebar. Review the radar chart showing your 8 personality traits. Read the Personality Overview card and Quick Stats panel. Scroll down to explore individual trait cards with action steps. Step 3 — Get your first Recommendations Click Recommendations in the sidebar. Click Refine Results to open the refinement panel. Choose your input mode: Select Test Scores if you have exam results to enter. Select Personality to use your trait profile directly. Fill in your data and click Apply All Filters. Review the ranked list of universities and programmes that match your profile. Image placeholder: Add screenshot of the recommendations results page with at least 3 ranked university cards visible after a filter has been applied.
4. Advisor First Run: Dashboard → Student Roster → First Notification Step 1 — Review your dashboard Sign in to the Advisor Panel. Look at the KPI widgets at the top — total students, pending forms, and recent activity. Check the Assigned Forms block for any forms requiring attention. Step 2 — View your student roster Click Students in the sidebar. Browse your roster — each row shows a student’s name, email, and latest activity signal. Click any student to open their full profile. Step 3 — Send your first notification Click Notifications in the sidebar. Click Create Notification. Write a welcome message and set the target to All Students. Click Send. Image placeholder: Add screenshot of the advisor dashboard first-load state highlighting the KPI widgets and sidebar.
5. Parent First Run: Link Your Child → View Their Progress Step 1 — Link your child’s account Sign in to the Parent Panel. On the dashboard, find the Link Student form. Enter the exact email address your child used to register. Click Send Link Request. Wait for your child to approve the request from their student dashboard. Step 2 — Open their profile Once linked, their card appears on your dashboard. Click View Student to open their full detail page. Review their assessment progress, recommendations summary, and advisor activity. Image placeholder: Add screenshot of the parent dashboard with a linked student card and the View Student button visible.
Common Questions Situation What to do I signed in but landed on the wrong panel Log out and use the correct login URL for your role — see Access URLs I clicked Start on the assessment but the page is blank Refresh the page. If it persists, try a different browser (Chrome or Firefox recommended) My child never received my link request Ask them to log in to their student dashboard and look for a notification about a pending parent link approval

body:
Assessments are the core of IDPicker. Completing them unlocks your personalized career paths, university recommendations, and personality insights.
1. Starting a New Assessment From the left sidebar, click Self Assessment. Look at the available assessment cards (e.g., Personality, Career). Note: Your school or organization may assign custom assessments here as well. Click Start on the card you want to begin. Read the question instructions carefully. Select or type your answer, then click Next. Repeat until all questions are answered, then click Submit. Tip: Do not overthink your answers, especially for personality tests. Your first instinct is usually the most accurate!
2. Resuming an Incomplete Assessment Life happens. If you close your browser or navigate away, your progress is safely auto-saved.
Return to the Self Assessment page (or click the Resume card on your Dashboard). The assessment card will now show a Continue button instead of Start. Click Continue. You will pick up exac servertly where you left off. Complete the remaining questions and submit. 3. Retaking an Assessment Some assessments allow you to retake them if you feel your situation has changed. If retakes are enabled for a specific test:
Open the completed assessment card. Click Retake. A confirmation prompt will ask if you are sure you want to overwrite your previous results. Confirm to proceed. Complete the new run and submit. 4. Reviewing Your Results Once you submit an assessment, the platform instantly generates your results.
On the completed assessment card, click View Results. Review the summary and breakdown of your traits or aptitudes. Armed with this new data, head over to the Recommendations or Personality modules to see what the AI has unlocked for you! 5. Important Note on Languages If you change your interface language (e.g., from English to Turkish) while an assessment is in progress, the platform may require you to confirm a reset of your current in-progress responses to match the new language schema. It is best to stick to one language until you submit!
Common Questions Situation What to do Recommendations or Personality menus are locked You must complete your core assessments first to unlock these areas The Start button is missing on a custom card The assessment may be closed, completed, or outside your organization’s allowed time window I lost progress unexpectedly Check if you switched languages mid-test. Otherwise, ensure you have a stable network connection

title: Assessment

body:
The Recommendations page is the heart of the IDPicker platform. Once your assessments are complete, the AI generates a personalized list of universities or career paths that match your profile. You can then sharpen those results in two powerful ways: by feeding in your real exam scores and rankings, or by leaning entirely on your completed personality profile. This guide walks through every option step by step.
1. Opening Your Recommendations In the left sidebar, click Recommendations. The page loads your results and displays a summary banner — for example, “Found 3 personalized recommendations” — along with a pass counter (e.g. 1/2) that tracks how many refinement passes have been applied. Scroll down to see your university or career cards ranked by match strength. Click any card title to open its full detail view, which explains precisely why the AI selected it for your profile. Tip: The pass counter next to the summary banner increments each time you apply a new set of filters. Use it to compare how your results change between refinement passes.
2. Choosing a Recommendation Input Mode Before the AI refines your results, you must tell it what data to prioritise. Click Refine Results (top-right button) to open the refinement panel, then select one of the two Recommendation input modes:
Mode What it uses Best for Test Scores Your entrance exam names, scores, and rankings Students who have sat official exams and know their results Personality Your completed personality assessment on IDPicker Students who want a purely trait-based match without exam data Click the tile for the mode you want — the selected tile gets a highlighted border.
3. Refining With Test Scores (Exam-Based Mode) This mode lets you enter the entrance exams you have actually sat, together with your score and ranking. Universities use these figures as admission criteria, so providing them produces the most accurate shortlist.
3a. How the Score Palette Works IDPicker supports up to 3 exam slots (shown as 1/3 in the panel). Each slot captures:
Exam name — the official name of the test (e.g. IELTS, SAT, TOEFL) Score — the numeric result you received Ranking (optional) — your position or percentile, e.g. Top 5% or 1000th Design tip shown in the panel: Blend aptitude, language, and speciality exams for a balanced story. For example, adding both a language exam (IELTS) and an aptitude exam (SAT) gives the AI richer data to match against university requirements.
3b. Adding Your First Exam (Step by Step) In the refinement panel, confirm Test Scores is selected. You will see a numbered row labelled 1 with three input fields: Exam name, Score, and Ranking (optional). Click the Exam name field and type the name of your exam (e.g. YKS-SAY). Click the Score field and enter your numeric score. Click the Ranking (optional) field and enter your rank if known (e.g. Top 5% or 15000th). 3c. Adding a Second or Third Exam Click the + button on the right side of the palette row counter. A new numbered row appears. Fill in Exam name, Score, and Ranking for the additional exam. Repeat for a third exam if needed (maximum 3 slots). Note: The + button is disabled once all 3 slots are filled. To replace an exam, clear an existing row first.
3d. Real-World Example — Turkish YKS Exam Turkey uses the YKS university entrance system, which includes several sub-tests. A typical student entry looks like this:
Field Example Value Exam name YKS – SAY (Science) Score 487.32 Ranking 8500th The SAY sub-score is the figure Turkish universities use to rank applicants for science and engineering faculties. Entering it gives IDPicker the exact criterion those universities apply, so the recommendation list mirrors real admission eligibility.
Other common Turkish exam entries:
YKS – EA (Equally Weighted) — used for economics, law, business YKS – SÖZ (Verbal) — used for humanities and social sciences YKS – DİL (Language) — used for foreign language degree programmes 3e. Applying Your Scores After filling in all your exam rows, click Apply All Filters (bottom-right of the refinement panel). The platform saves your score palette and triggers a new recommendation pass. The pass counter in the summary banner increments (e.g. from 1/2 to 2/2). Your ranked list updates to reflect universities whose admission criteria your scores meet. 4. Refining With Personality (Trait-Based Mode) If you have completed the Personality assessment on IDPicker, you can let the AI use your psychological trait scores instead of exam data.
In the refinement panel, click the Personality tile. The exam score fields disappear — no manual input is required. Click Apply All Filters. The AI re-ranks your recommendations based on trait compatibility with each university or career programme. Note: This mode requires a completed Personality assessment. If you have not finished it, the tile may be locked. Go to Assessment first.
5. Filtering by Country The Refine by Country dropdown narrows results to universities located in a specific country before the AI applies its ranking logic.
In the refinement panel, click the Select country dropdown under Refine by:. Scroll or type to find your target country. Select it — the dropdown updates to show the chosen country. Click Apply All Filters to reload results for that country only. Tip: Combine country filtering with an exam mode for the tightest shortlist. For example, selecting Turkey + entering YKS–SAY scores surfaces only Turkish universities that accept your specific score range.
6. Filtering by City After country-level filtering, you can drill down further by city using the Filter by city control that appears above the results list.
Scroll past the refinement panel to the Filter by city row. Click the city dropdown (defaults to All cities). Select the city you want — only universities in that city will show. Note: City-level filtering is applied independently of the refinement panel. You do not need to click Apply All Filters again — city changes take effect immediately.
7. Reading the Recommendation Cards Each university card in your results list displays key admission and programme information at a glance:
Element What it shows University name Full official name including city and type (state/private) Premium badge Marks universities available to Premium-tier subscribers only Programme details Faculty, degree type, and language of instruction Match indicators Visual signals showing how closely the university fits your profile Click the university name to open its full detail page showing all programmes, requirements, and the AI’s reasoning for the match.
8. Downloading Your Full Report If your account plan supports it, you can export your recommendation profile as a PDF.
Look for the Download My Report button at the top of the recommendations page. Click it and wait for the document to be prepared. A secure link opens in a new tab — click it to save the PDF. Note: If the button is hidden, report export is not enabled for your current plan or organisation policy. Check your subscription under Settings.
Common Questions Situation What to do The recommendations feel inaccurate Retake the assessment if you rushed through it, then apply filters again I want career results, not universities Use the Guidance Track switcher to change focus — see Assessment The Personality tile is locked Complete the Personality assessment first under the Assessment module I entered my YKS score but the list did not change Make sure you clicked Apply All Filters after entering the score The + button to add more exams is disabled You have reached the 3-exam limit. Clear one row to replace it City filter shows no results The selected country may not have city-level data. Try All cities Report download failed Wait 60 seconds and try again — intermittent network drops can interrupt PDF generation Pass counter is stuck at 1/2 A failed filter apply may have stopped the count. Re-apply your filters and check your connection

title: Recommendation

body:
The Personality module reveals who you are through eight measured psychological traits. After completing the Personality assessment, you receive a full analysis: an interactive radar chart, a personality overview with strength tiers, quick stat rankings, and individual trait cards with detailed interpretations and concrete action steps. This page explains every section so you can use your results to guide your studies, career choices, and personal growth.
Warning — Take the assessment in a calm, focused environment. Your personality results are only as accurate as the honesty and mindfulness you bring to the questions. Find a quiet place, set aside at least 15–20 uninterrupted minutes, put your phone away, and answer each question based on how you actually behave — not how you wish you behaved. Rushed or distracted answers will skew your trait scores and reduce the quality of your university and career recommendations.
1. Prerequisite: Complete the Personality Assessment The Personality page is locked until you finish the assessment. If you see a locked state:
From the sidebar, click Assessment. Locate the Personality assessment card. Click Start and work through every question without skipping. Submit the assessment when all questions are complete. Return to the sidebar and click Personality — your full analysis is now unlocked. Note: Once you submit, results are generated instantly. You do not need to wait or refresh.
2. The Personality Insights Header At the top of the page you will see a Personality Insights badge and the heading Your Personality Analysis, followed by the subline:
“Explore your unique personality traits through the interactive radar chart below, based on your assessment.”
Below the heading is the Export button — this lets you download your full report at any time. See section 7 for full export steps.
3. Reading the Radar Chart The radar chart is the first visual you see after the header. It plots all eight traits simultaneously so you can grasp your overall profile shape at a glance.
The Eight Traits on the Chart Trait Axis position on chart What it measures Emotional Balance Top centre How steadily you manage your feelings under pressure Social Connection Right Your ability to build and sustain positive relationships Mental Agility Lower right Cognitive flexibility and capacity for clear thinking Inner Drive Lower right-centre Internal motivation and self-push to reach goals Self-Awareness Bottom centre Understanding of your own traits, behaviours, and impact Growth Mindset Lower left Openness to learning, challenges, and self-improvement Adaptive Thinking Left Thoughtfulness and balanced decision-making Goal Focus Upper left Clarity of vision and directed energy toward goals How to Read the Chart Look at the overall shape — a large, even polygon means broadly developed traits. A lopsided shape signals strong peaks and clear growth areas. Identify the outermost points — these are your Top Strengths (scores 7–10). Identify the innermost points — these are your Growth Areas (scores 1–5), which represent the biggest opportunities for self-improvement. Hover over any axis point to see the exact numeric score for that trait. 4. The Personality Overview Panel Directly below the radar chart, the Personality Overview panel gives a written summary of your profile organised into three tiers.
Tier Score range What it means Strong Traits Scores 7–10: Exceptional abilities These are your natural strengths — behaviours that come easily and can be leveraged for academic and career success Balanced Traits Scores 4–6: Well-developed skills Solid, reliable skills that with moderate attention can become strengths Growth Areas Scores 1–3: Development opportunities Traits that are currently underdeveloped and represent your highest-potential areas of improvement The overview card opens with a paragraph describing the unique pattern of your results and how your traits shape your approach to learning, relationships, and personal growth.
5. Quick Stats Panel To the right of the Personality Overview is the Quick Stats panel. It lists your highest-scoring traits under Top Strengths and your lowest-scoring traits under Growth Areas, each with their numeric score shown.
How to use Quick Stats:
Use the Top Strengths list to identify which traits to emphasise in application essays, interviews, and conversations with your advisor. Use the Growth Areas list to prioritise which Action Steps (from the trait cards below) to work on first. 6. Individual Trait Cards Below the overview section, every one of the eight traits has its own card. Each card follows the same structure and tells you exactly what your score means and what to do about it.
Card Anatomy Element What it shows Trait name The name of the measured trait Score bar A filled progress bar representing 1–10 scale Score number Your exact numeric score Level label Colour-coded label: Exceptional (green), Strong (green), Moderate (orange) One-liner A single sentence summary of what the score says about you Detailed Analysis A paragraph-length explanation of how this trait manifests in your daily life, study habits, and decision-making Action Steps One or two concrete, practical things you can do right now to build on a strength or develop a growth area The Eight Trait Cards in Detail Growth Mindset Your openness to learning, challenges, and self-improvement. A high score here means you see setbacks as fuel for growth rather than failure.
What to do with a high score: Keep seeking out challenging courses, new skills, and experiences that push you past your comfort zone.
What to do with a low score: Start small — pick one new skill or subject per month and commit to practicing it even when it feels difficult.
Emotional Balance How well you regulate your emotions and stay steady under pressure. A high score means you can handle stress, disappointment, and uncertainty without being derailed.
What to do with a high score: Use this stability to support peers and take on leadership roles in group projects or student activities.
What to do with a low score: Build a small daily routine (journaling, breathing exercises, short walks) that gives your emotions a healthy outlet before pressure builds.
Goal Focus The clarity of your vision and how directed your energy is toward specific targets. A high score means you rarely waste effort on distractions.
What to do with a high score: Write down both short-term and long-term goals and review them weekly to stay calibrated.
What to do with a low score: Start with one concrete weekly goal and practise completing it before adding more. Clarity comes from doing, not planning.
Social Connection Your capacity to build meaningful, positive relationships. A high score means you connect easily and bring warmth to group settings.
What to do with a high score: Use your social strength to join new groups, clubs, or communities aligned with your career interests — connections open doors.
What to do with a low score: Pick one low-stakes social activity per week (a study group, an online forum, a class) to practise connection in a comfortable environment.
Inner Drive Your internal motivation and the push you generate from within. A high score means you do not need external pressure to stay productive.
What to do with a high score: Break bigger goals into smaller milestones — your drive compounds fastest when you can see forward progress regularly.
What to do with a low score: Identify what genuinely excites you (not what you think should excite you) and anchor your daily tasks to that.
Adaptive Thinking Your thoughtfulness and ability to consider multiple options before acting. A high score means you make balanced decisions and avoid impulsive choices.
What to do with a high score: Practise applying this skill to everyday decisions — even small daily choices build the habit of measured thinking.
What to do with a low score: Before acting on a decision, write down two alternative options and weigh the trade-offs. The habit takes three to four weeks to form.
Mental Agility Your cognitive flexibility, learning speed, and capacity for clear thinking. A high score means you process new information efficiently and adapt quickly to new environments.
What to do with a high score: Challenge yourself with problem-solving activities — maths puzzles, strategy games, or learning a new language keeps this trait sharp.
What to do with a low score: Break complex tasks into the smallest possible steps. Cognitive load goes down when you focus on one piece at a time.
Self-Awareness How clearly you understand your own traits, habits, emotional triggers, and impact on others. A high score means your self-perception aligns with how others actually experience you.
What to do with a high score: Continue reflecting on your experiences — journalling, asking for honest feedback, and reviewing your decisions weekly deepens this further.
What to do with a low score: After any significant interaction or decision, take five minutes to ask yourself: What did I do well? What would I change? Small reflection habits compound over time.
Image placeholder: Add screenshot showing two Growth Area trait cards side by side (e.g., Mental Agility and Self-Awareness) each with score bars and action steps visible.
7. Exporting Your Personality Report Your full personality analysis can be exported as a PDF to share with parents, advisors, or for personal records.
Click the Export button at the top of the Personality page (below the header). Wait a few seconds while the document is generated. A secure link opens in a new tab — click it to download your PDF report. Note: If the Export button is missing or disabled, report export may not be enabled for your current subscription plan or organisation policy. Check Settings → Subscription or contact your advisor.
Image placeholder: Add screenshot showing the Export button at the top of the page and the resulting PDF open in a new browser tab.
8. Using Your Personality Results With Other Platform Features Your personality data does not exist in isolation — it feeds directly into other IDPicker modules:
Module How personality connects Recommendations Select Personality mode in the Refine panel to match universities and careers to your trait profile instead of exam scores — see Recommendations AI Mentorship Your active AI mentor is contextually aware of your personality scores and tailors its guidance accordingly Advisor Chat Share your Quick Stats with your advisor to focus your next guidance session on specific Growth Areas Common Questions Situation What to do The Personality page is locked Complete the Personality assessment in the Assessment module first My scores feel wrong or don’t reflect me You may have answered quickly or in a distracted state. Retake the assessment in a calm, focused setting The radar chart loads but trait cards are blank A network drop occurred mid-load. Reload the page I want to retake the assessment Go to Assessment, open the Personality card, and click Retake if the option is available The Export button is missing Report export may not be enabled for your plan. Check Settings or ask your advisor My Growth Mindset score is high but recommendations still feel off Personality mode in Recommendations uses all eight trait scores together. Try combining personality mode with country or exam filters for sharper results

body:
The Mentor Hub is your central communication space. Here, you can seek advice from specialized AI personalities or chat directly with your assigned human advisor.
1. Starting a Chat with an AI Mentor IDPicker provides multiple AI personalities, each tuned for specific types of advice (e.g., strict academic planning, empathetic career counseling, technical skills building).
In the sidebar, click Mentor. Browse the grid of available mentor cards to find a personality that fits your current question. Click Select/Open on your preferred mentor. Once the chat interface opens, type your question into the message box at the bottom. Click the Send icon. The AI will respond in real-time, contextually aware of your personality and recommendation profile! Quota Warning: Depending on your subscription, you may have a chat quota. A progress bar usually indicates how many messages you have left. Use your prompts wisely!
2. Resuming a Recent Chat You do not have to start over every time.
In the Mentor Hub, look for the Recent Chats list. Click on a recent thread. The chat history will load, and you can pick up the conversation exactly where you left off. 3. Chatting With Your Human Advisor If your account is linked to an organization or school, your human advisor manages your progress. You can message them directly here.
On the main Mentor page, look for the Advisor section. You will see your advisor’s name, last known activity, and a red badge if you have unread messages. Click the Chat with Advisor button. Type and send your message. When your advisor replies, you will be notified on your dashboard. Note: If the advisor section says “No advisor linked,” your account operates independently and this feature is disabled.
Common Questions Situation What to do I clicked a mentor but the chat didn’t open Refresh the page and try again; verify your network is stable The Advisor chat button is greyed out You do not currently have a human advisor linked to your account My messages stopped sending You have likely hit your chat quota limit. Check your subscription settings

body:
The University Search module is your comprehensive database for discovering higher education programs worldwide. This guide explains how to use the search engine efficiently to build your target list.
1. Basic Keyword Search In the sidebar, click University Search. Wait a moment for the filter panel and the default results area to load. Click the Search input box at the top. Type a university name (e.g., Harvard) or a program keyword (e.g., Computer Science). Pause briefly — the search runs automatically as you type to prevent unnecessary reloading. Review your refined results. 2. Power Filtering To drill down into specific criteria, use the filter panel on the left (or in the top dropdowns, depending on your device).
1. By Category (Field of Study) Click the Category dropdown and select your overarching field, such as Engineering, Arts, or Medicine. The results will instantly filter to programs mapped to that category.
2. By Degree Type Looking for a Bachelor’s, Master’s, or PhD? Click the Degree dropdown to restrict results to exactly what you need.
3. By Language of Instruction If you want to study entirely in English in a non-English speaking country, this filter is life-saving. Select the instruction language to filter out programs you cannot attend.
Warning: Mixing too many strict filters at once (e.g., Aerospace Engineering, PhD, Turkish language, in Germany) will likely return zero results. If you get an empty page, clear your filters one by one until results reappear.
3. Managing Pagination (Load More) When reviewing results:
Scroll to the bottom of the current page of university cards. Click Load More (or use the pagination numbers 1, 2, 3…) to fetch the next set. You can change how many cards load at once by tweaking the Page Size control (e.g., 6, 12, 24). Note: Any time you apply a new filter or type a new keyword, you are automatically bumped back to Page 1 so you do not miss top results.
4. How to Compare Programs Efficiently When building your shortlist, we recommend focusing on these core factors displayed on every card:
University Name & Program Name Degree Level Language of Instruction Tuition Cost (if available) Duration (years) Country / City Keep a notebook or spreadsheet open alongside IDPicker to save your top targets!
Common Questions Situation What to do The search bar feels laggy Input is deliberately “debounced” (delayed) by a few milliseconds so it does not overwhelm the server while you type. Just pause when finished! My results completely disappeared You probably applied an overly strict combination of filters The card order suddenly changed When you apply a new filter, a brand new sorted list is retrieved from the database

The Resources module acts as your personal digital library. Whenever your advisor, school, or organization shares a helpful document, spreadsheet, or PDF, it lands here.
1. Browsing the Resource Library From the main sidebar, click Resources. Wait a second for your personalized file grid to load. You will immediately see files that are explicitly assigned to you, as well as globally visible files shared by your organization. Searching by Keyword Know exactly what you are looking for?
Click the Search bar. Type a matching keyword from the file’s title, description, or original file name. The list will instantly filter to matching documents. 2. Filtering and Sorting Files If your organization has shared hundreds of files, use these controls to narrow them down:
File Type Filter: Only want to see PDFs or Excel sheets? Select the exact format. Size Filter: Select Small, Medium, or Large files (helpful if you are on a slow cellular connection). Visibility Filter: Choose between All, Globally Visible, or Directly Assigned. Sort By: Change the ordering from Date Added, Alphabetical Name, or File Size. View Mode: Toggle between the spacious Grid view or the compact List view depending on your preference. 3. Downloading a Shared File Locate the card or row of the file you need. Click the Download button. Your browser will handle the secure download. Check your computer’s Downloads folder when it finishes. If a download fails: Check your internet connection first. If it still fails, refresh the entire page to ensure your login session has not quietly expired, then try downloading again.
Common Questions Situation What to do My advisor said they shared a file, but I cannot see it They may have forgotten to assign it directly to your email. Ask them to verify the visibility settings on their end. The file list keeps shuffling when I click things The Sort By option applies instantly whenever a filter changes A file card is missing a description Advisors are not strictly required to type out extensive descriptions when uploading

body:
Sometimes things break or you encounter a feature you do not understand. The Help Center is your direct line to the IDPicker platform support team.
1. Opening a New Support Ticket In the sidebar, click Help Center. Make sure the New Ticket tab is selected. Click the Subject line and type a brief, clear summary (e.g., Cannot download personality report). Select the matching Category (Technical, Account, Billing, Feature, General, or Other). Select a Priority (Low, Medium, High, Urgent). Note: Please only use Urgent for complete account blockers (like being totally unable to log in). In the Description box, provide as much detail as possible. Click Submit and wait for the success flash. 2. How to Write a Ticket That Gets Fixed Fast The support team wants to help, but they cannot see your screen. Provide these details to speed up resolution:
Exactly what page or module you were on. The exact text of any error message shown. What you clicked before the error happened. What you expected to happen, vs what actually happened. 3. Tracking Your Submitted Tickets Once submitted, you can monitor the status of your request without sending duplicate emails.
Click the My Tickets tab at the top of the Help Center. Find the issue in your list. Review the Status badge to understand current progress: Status Badge What it Means Pending Received, sitting in the queue waiting for an agent In Progress A support agent is actively investigating right now Resolved The agent has implemented a fix or provided an answer Closed The issue is fully completed and archived If an agent replies requiring more info, you will be notified!
Common Questions Situation What to do I clicked submit but got a red error Check that you actually wrote a Subject and Description. Both are required! I submitted my ticket but my list is empty You are probably still on the New Ticket tab. Switch back to My Tickets What category do I pick for login issues? Select Account, unless you are specifically facing a payment/subscription issue, then pick Billing
description: How to create support tickets and get help when things go wrong

body:
IDPicker keeps you informed through a built-in notification system. Whenever your advisor sends you a message, assigns you a form, or an action requires your attention, a notification appears in your inbox so nothing falls through the cracks.
1. Opening Your Notification Inbox Look for the Bell icon in the top navigation bar. A red badge on the bell shows how many unread notifications you have. Click the bell to open the Notification Dropdown — this shows your five most recent messages. Click View All Notifications at the bottom of the dropdown to open the full inbox page. 2. Reading a Notification In the full notifications list, unread items appear with a highlighted background or a blue dot indicator. Click any notification row to open it. The detail view shows: Title of the notification Full message body — may include instructions or links to take action Sender (e.g., your advisor’s name, or IDPicker System) Timestamp — when it was sent After you open it, the notification is automatically marked as Read and the unread badge count decreases. 3. Marking All as Read If you have many unread notifications and want to clear the badge quickly:
On the full notifications list page, click Mark All as Read. All notification rows are instantly marked as read. The red badge on the bell icon disappears. Note: Marking notifications as read does not delete them. They remain in your list for reference.
4. Types of Notifications Type What it means What to do Advisor Message Your advisor sent you a note or update Read the message and reply in the Chat module if needed Form Assigned An advisor or admin has added a new form for you to complete Click the notification to go directly to the form and fill it in Assessment Reminder A scheduled or overdue assessment needs your attention Click through to open the assessment and complete it Parent Link Request Received A parent has requested to view your account Go to your Account Settings to approve or deny the link request Certificate Issued Your advisor has issued you a new certificate Find it in the Certificates and Documents section to download Application Document Update Your advisor reviewed and updated the status of a document you uploaded Check your Application Documents section for feedback 5. Acting on a Notification Most notifications include a direct action link inside the message body. Click the link to jump straight to the relevant page — for example, clicking a Form Assigned notification takes you directly to the form’s fill screen.
If a notification does not have a direct link, use the sidebar navigation to go to the relevant module manually.
Common Questions Situation What to do My bell shows a badge count but I cannot find unread notifications Open the full inbox and look for the blue dot or highlighted row. The count may include system-level notices that appear without a visible highlight in all browsers I accidentally marked everything as read before seeing a message The notifications are still in your list. Scroll through and look for any you have not acted on yet I am not receiving notifications about my forms Check with your advisor to confirm the form was assigned to your account specifically. Also verify your notifications settings have not been muted

This guide explains how students identify free-plan limits and use upgrade paths.
1. Understand Feature Limits Free-plan limits can apply to:
Mentor chat usage Assessment/recommendation usage counters Full report download availability 2. Where Students See Limit Signals Open a quota-sensitive module (Mentor, Assessments, Recommendations). Locate quota indicator or blocked state. Read remaining usage and limit label. 3. Upgrade Flow from Feature Context Reach a gated/limited action. Click Upgrade or equivalent CTA. Follow the presented flow for plan upgrade. Note:
Dedicated subscription page availability may vary by deployment. In-context upgrade prompts are currently the reliable path. 4. What to Do When Subscription Menu Exists but Page Is Missing Stay in module where limit appears. Use in-module upgrade CTA. Confirm post-upgrade access after completion. Common Student Questions Q: I can read recommendation cards but cannot download full report. A: Full report export can be plan/availability controlled.
Q: I see subscription in sidebar but route fails. A: Use in-module upgrade actions until dedicated route is enabled in your deployment.
Q: How do I check remaining usage? A: Check quota indicators shown in quota-controlled features.

body:
This guide explains how students edit profile, manage parent links, switch guidance track, and delete account safely.
1. Edit Profile (Click-by-Click) In sidebar, click Profile. Click Edit Profile. Update fields as needed: First name Last name Bio Location Job title Gender Instagram URL X URL Click Save. Wait for success confirmation. If save fails: Recheck required fields and URL formats. Retry once after page refresh. 2. Switch Recommendation Track from Settings In sidebar, click Settings. Locate Guidance Track section. Click switch action (Career/University). Wait for saved confirmation. Open Recommendations and confirm new mode. 3. Link Parent Account Stay on Settings page. Locate Parent Linking section. Click parent email input. Enter parent email. Click Send/Request Link. Wait for confirmation. Possible outcomes: Linked immediately Pending until parent-side confirmation flow 4. Remove Parent Access In Parent Links list, locate linked parent row. Click Unlink. Confirm action. Verify parent is removed from linked list. 5. Delete Student Account (High Risk) In Settings, open Delete Account area. Click Delete Account. Read warning carefully. Enter required confirmation word exactly. Click final delete confirmation. Wait for logout/redirect. Best practice: Export/save anything needed before deletion. Common Student Questions Q: Parent link request sent but not linked yet. A: Link may stay pending until parent confirmation path is completed.
Q: Track switched in settings but recommendations unchanged. A: Re-open Recommendations or refresh once.
Q: Delete button did not proceed. A: Confirmation word is strict; type exactly as shown.

body:
As an Advisor, your platform access is tailored to your operational role. This guide covers how to get logged in securely and how to interpret your main dashboard.
1. Advisor Login Depending on your organization’s setup, you will access the platform either via a custom organization link or the main advisor portal. (See Access URLs if you are unsure).
Open the Advisor Login page. Enter your registered Email and Password. Click Sign In. The system initializes your specific role and permission set, then drops you onto your Dashboard. Why is my dashboard missing certain modules? If you are a “Sub-Advisor,” your access is strictly limited to the permissions your primary Advisor delegated to you. If you cannot see a module, you do not have permission for it.
2. Setting Up Your Account (First Time) If you were invited to join as an advisor or sub-advisor by your organization lead:
Check your email for an invitation from IDPicker. Click the Setup Password link in that email. You will be taken to a secure token-validation page. Enter a strong new password, confirm it, and submit to activate your account. Return directly to the login page and sign in. 3. Password Reset Flow If you have forgotten your password:
On the login page, click Forgot Password. Enter your advisor email address. You will receive a reset link. Click it, set a new password, and verify the strength indicator. If the link says it is expired, immediately request a new one from the login page. 4. Understanding Your Dashboard The Advisor Dashboard serves as your daily command center.
KPI Widgets (Top Row): These blocks show quick metrics regarding your linked students, such as total student count, recently completed assessments, and pending forms. Assigned Forms Block: Immediately see which of your forms currently have pending responses from students. Main Navigation (Sidebar): Use the sidebar to quickly jump between managing Students, organizing Groups, distributing Resources, or handling Support/Chat. Tip: If your platform supports multiple languages, use the language toggle in the header to switch your interface language instantly.
Image placeholder: Add screenshot of the full advisor dashboard with KPI widgets at the top and the sidebar modules listed on the left.
Common Questions Situation What to do I logged in but cannot see the “Groups” module Your account is likely a Sub-Advisor with restricted permissions My dashboard looks partially loaded or broken Wait a few seconds for all underlying API calls to finish, then refresh the page once The password setup link in my email doesn’t work Security tokens expire quickly. Ask your administrator to resend the invitation, or use the “Forgot Password” flow if the account was already created

The Students module is your CRM for managing the individuals you advise. You can view their progress individually, assign them to groups, or import hundreds of students at once.
1. Searching and Filtering Your Roster When you open the Students module from the sidebar, you see your entire authorized roster.
Search: Use the search bar to find a student quickly by name or email. Filter: Narrow down your list using the drop-down filters. You can filter by: Assigned Groups Chat status (e.g., awaiting reply) Assessment signals (e.g., completed, missing) Joined date or location Sort: Click the column headers (Name, Email, Joined Date) to reorder the list. Viewing Student Details Click on any student row or card to open their detailed profile. Here you get full context on their background, activities, and current plateau.
2. Managing Group Assignments To help organize your communications, you can assign students to Groups right from the roster.
From the Students list, select the checkbox next to one or more students. Click the Assign to Group action button. Check the boxes for the target groups. Click Save. The membership updates instantly. (Note: See the Groups Management guide for how to create groups in the first place).
3. Bulk Importing Students (Excel) If you have a large cohort starting simultaneously, do not add them one by one. Use the Bulk Import tool.
On the Students page, click Bulk Actions > Import. Prepare your .xlsx or .xls file. Your spreadsheet must contain these three exact column headers in the first row: email first_name last_name Upload the file. The system will parse the file and show a Preview Table. Select the rows you wish to actually import, or select all. Click Confirm Import. Wait. The system will summarize successes and any skipped rows (failures). Important Import Rules File Limits: There is a maximum file size limit (usually a few megabytes). Duplicates: If an email already exists in the system, that row will be skipped/flagged. Group Assignment: Imported students are not automatically placed in a group upon import. You must assign them afterward. Common Questions Situation What to do I uploaded an Excel file but the preview is totally blank Make sure your file is a valid .xlsx and exactly matches the required lowercase header names (email, first_name, last_name) The filters say “0 students found” but I know they exist You likely have conflicting filters applied. Clear all filters and reapply them one by one I just imported students; how do I message them all? Create a Group for the new cohort, assign them, and then use the Notifications module to broadcast a message

This guide covers two closely related features: issuing platform-generated certificates to recognise student achievements, and reviewing the application documents students upload as part of their university or programme applications.
1. Issuing a Certificate Certificates are formal, advisor-generated recognitions that appear in the student’s Certificates module and can be downloaded as PDFs.
In the sidebar, click Certificates. Click Issue Certificate. A certificate builder dialog opens. Fill in: Student — Search and select the recipient. Certificate Type — Choose from available templates (e.g., Assessment Completion, Programme Readiness, Participation). Issue Date — Defaults to today; change if back-dating is required. Notes (Optional) — Add a personalised message that will appear on the certificate document. Click Issue. The certificate is immediately added to the student’s account. They will see it in their Certificates and Documents section and receive a notification. Note: Once a certificate is issued, it cannot be edited. If you made an error, you must revoke the certificate and reissue a corrected one.
Image placeholder: Add screenshot of the certificate builder dialog with all fields filled in before clicking Issue.(Verify)
2. Reviewing Issued Certificates To keep track of what has been issued:
On the Certificates page, you see a table of all certificates you have issued. Filter by Student Name or Certificate Type to narrow the list. Each row shows the issue date, student name, type, and current status (Active or Revoked). Click any row to open the certificate detail and preview the generated document. 3. Revoking a Certificate If a certificate was issued by mistake or needs to be corrected:
Find the certificate in your issued list. Click the action menu (...) next to it and select Revoke. Confirm the revocation prompt. The certificate is immediately marked as Revoked and disappears from the student’s active certificate list. Warning: Revoking a certificate cannot be undone for audit trail purposes. If you need to correct an error, revoke the original and issue a new one.
4. Reviewing Student Application Documents Students upload supporting documents (transcripts, passports, language certificates) as part of their application process. Your job is to verify, flag, or reject each uploaded file.
In the sidebar, click Application Documents. The list shows all students who have active document collections, each with a completion progress bar. Click a student row to open their Document Collection. Image placeholder: Add screenshot of the application documents list showing several student rows with their progress bars.
5. Verifying Individual Documents Inside a student’s collection, you see a checklist of required document steps. Click on a specific step (e.g., High School Transcript). The uploaded file appears — click Preview or Download to inspect it. Set the review status: Verified — The document is valid and meets requirements. Pending — You need more time to verify, or you are waiting for additional context. Invalid — The document is incorrect, expired, or does not meet requirements. If you mark the file Invalid, fill in the Reason field with a clear explanation so the student knows exactly what to fix. Click Save Review. Image placeholder: Add screenshot of the document review panel for a single document step — show the file preview area, status dropdown, and reason text field.
6. Completing a Collection Once all individual document steps are verified:
Return to the collection overview for the student. Confirm all steps show a green Verified badge. Click Approve Collection to formally mark the student’s application document set as complete. The student receives a notification that their documents have been approved. Common Questions Situation What to do A student says they uploaded a file but I cannot see it Refresh the collection detail page — if it still does not appear, verify the student completed the upload step rather than just selecting the file I need to leave a note for a student about a rejected document Use the Reason field when setting a step to Invalid. The student can read this note in their own document panel I issued a certificate with the wrong date Revoke the original certificate immediately and re-issue a new one with the correct date

This guide covers three distinct operational flows that advisors handle daily: approving new students, managing multidirectional chats, and setting up organizational assessments.
1. Managing Enrollment Requests Depending on your organization’s configuration, students who register may not join your roster automatically. They may land in an approval queue.
In the sidebar, click Enrollment Requests. A list of pending student accounts will appear. Click into a request to review the individual’s profile to verify they belong to your organization. Click Approve to admit them (which grants them full platform access and adds them to your Students roster). Click Deny if they do not belong. Image placeholder: Add screenshot of the enrollment requests queue showing pending student cards with Approve and Deny buttons.
2. Using Family Chat IDPicker recognizes that guidance is a collaborative effort between the advisor, the student, and the parents.
In the sidebar, click Family Chat (or navigate to the Chat module). You will see different message threads categorized by scope: Student Threads: Direct 1-on-1 chats with the student. Parent Threads: Direct chats with the linked parent. Family Threads: Multi-party threads involving both the student and the parent(s). Select a thread, type your message in the composer, and hit Send. A red badge will appear next to names when they reply. Tip: If a parent thread is disabled, it means the student has not yet approved a parent linkage request on their end.
Image placeholder: Add screenshot of the family chat panel showing the different thread types and an open conversation.
3. Custom Assessments While IDPicker provides built-in psychological and career assessments, your organization may want to issue bespoke quizzes (e.g., a “Math Placement Exam” or “Mid-Year Feedback”).
In the sidebar, click Assessments. Click Create Assessment (or clone an existing template). Build your questions (Multiple Choice, Rating Scales, Short Answer). Configure the visibility parameters (e.g., Which groups get this? Does it expire?). Publish the assessment. It will immediately appear on the targeted students’ Self Assessment dashboard. Common Questions Situation What to do The Approve/Deny buttons in Enrollment are missing Your account might be a restricted sub-advisor, or the request is already processed. Check the “Resolved” tab I sent a family chat but the parent did not reply Parents receive email notifications about unread messages, but they still have to log in to read them. Mention it during a phone call I need to fix a typo in a Published Assessment Once an assessment starts receiving answers, structural edits may be locked. Fix typos immediately before distributing, or duplicate the assessment and pause the old one

The Forms module allows advisors to create custom questionnaires, surveys, or data-collection sheets, distribute them to specific students, and track the responses in one place.
1. Understanding the Forms Lifecycle Every form moves through a specific lifecycle:
Draft: The form is being built and is invisible to students. Published: The form is actively collecting data and is visible to assigned students on their dashboard. Archived: (If applicable) The form is no longer accepting new responses, but old data is preserved. 2. Creating and Publishing a Form In the sidebar, click Forms. Click Create New Form. Enter a Title and a helpful Description. Use the form builder interface to add your specific fields (e.g., text inputs, dropdowns, checkboxes). Click Save as Draft while you are still working on it. When you are completely finished, select your target audience (groups or specific students) and change the status to Publish. Preview Before Publishing Always click the Preview action before publishing! This lets you see the form exactly as a student will see it, ensuring that your required fields and visual layout behave as expected.
3. Managing Existing Forms You can manage your forms from the main Forms list view:
Search & Filter: Use the search bar to find a specific form by title, or filter to see only Drafts or Published forms. Edit: If a form is still a draft, you can freely edit its structure. Once an active form begins receiving responses, structural edits may be heavily restricted to prevent data corruption. Delete: Select the delete action to permanently remove the form. Note: Doing this may also delete associated response records. 4. Reviewing Student Responses Once your form is published and students begin filling it out, you need to review their answers.
Locate the active form in your Forms list. You will see a counter indicating the number of Total Responses. Click the Responses button. A table will load showing a list of every student who submitted the form and the timestamp. Click on an individual response row to drill down and read the specific answers provided by that student. Common Questions Situation What to do A student says they submitted, but my response count is not updating The interface does not automatically live-refresh. Manually refresh the page to load the latest data from the server The system prevents me from editing a form The form is either locked due to your permission policy, or it is locked because it is Published and actively receiving responses I accidentally deleted a form Form deletion is permanent. Always export response data before taking destructive actions

Groups are an essential organizational tool. By grouping students—whether by graduation year, specific study tracks, or custom campaign objectives—you can distribute forms, share resources, and send notifications much more efficiently.
1. Creating a New Group In the sidebar, click Groups. Wait for the current groups list to load. Click the Create Group button. Enter a clear, descriptive Group Name (e.g., “Class of 2026 - Engineering Track”). (Optional) Add a short description for your own internal reference. A student selector will appear. Select the specific students you want to add to this group right now. Click Save. Your new group appears in the list. Best Practices for Naming Groups Be Consistent: Use a standard naming convention (e.g., [Year] - [Major/Track] - [Cohort]). Be Specific: “Group A” becomes impossible to manage after a few months. Review Often: Clean up and delete groups when a cohort graduates or finishes a campaign. 2. Editing an Existing Group Need to rename a group or add/remove members?
Find the target group in the list and click to open it. Click Edit. Update the name, description, or adjust the member checkmarks. Click Save to apply the changes instantly. Note: You can also add students directly to groups from the Students List by using the bulk action menu.
3. Deleting a Group When a group is no longer needed:
Open the target group or click its action menu (...) in the list view. Click Delete. A confirmation prompt will ask if you are sure. Click Confirm. Review the roster to verify the group is gone. Warning: Deleting a group does not delete the student accounts inside it. It only removes the organizational tag grouping them together.
Image placeholder: Add screenshot of the groups list showing several group cards or rows with member count badges and action menus.
Common Questions Situation What to do I cannot find a specific student to add to a group Check your permission scope; if you are a sub-advisor, you can only group students you have explicitly been granted access to The Delete button is grayed out or missing Your sub-advisor permissions do not allow you to delete groups. You can only view or manage members I saved the group but the member count looks wrong Re-open the edit dialog and verify that you actually checked the boxes next to the student names before hitting save

The Notifications module is your mass-communication tool. Instead of sending hundreds of individual emails, use notifications to broadcast platform-native announcements, reminders, and alerts directly to student dashboards.
1. Creating a New Broadcast In the sidebar, click Notifications. You will see a history of your past broadcasts. Click Create Notification. Enter a clear, attention-grabbing Title. Type your Message Body. Keep it concise but ensure all necessary links or action items are included. Select your Target Type: All Students: Broadcasts to every single student in your organization. Use sparingly. Selected Users: Allows you to hand-pick individuals. Selected Groups: The most common option. Targets a pre-made cohort (e.g., “Seniors”). If you chose Users or Groups, a selection interface will appear. Pick your targets. Click Send (or Create). The notification is immediately dispatched and will appear in your history list. 2. Reviewing Delivery and Effectiveness Unlike email, platform notifications allow you to track engagement. In your notification history list, you can verify:
Target Type: A quick reminder of who received the broadcast. Created Date: Exactly when it went out. Total Recipients: The absolute number of unique students who received the ping. Read Count: How many students have clicked on the notification to read the full body. Unread Count: How many students have ignored or not yet seen the alert. 3. Filtering Your Broadcast History If you need to check what you sent months ago:
Apply the Target Type filters (e.g., show me all messages sent to “All Students”). Use the search input to find a specific keyword or campaign title. Review the historical Read Counts to determine which types of subject lines get the most engagement from your roster. Common Questions Situation What to do The read rate is extremely low Give it 24 hours. Students only see notifications when they actively log in to their dashboard I accidentally sent a notification to the wrong group Platform notifications are instant and generally cannot be un-sent. Send a follow-up correction immediately to the same wrong group The “Selected Groups” option is greyed out You cannot target groups if you have not created any groups yet. See the Groups guide
